([/ˈtʌskəni/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*TUSK-ə-nee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Toscana* [[tosˈkaːna]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian")) is an administrative [region](/wiki/Regions_of_Italy "Regions of Italy") of [central Italy](/wiki/Central_Italy "Central Italy") with an area of about 23,000 square kilometres (8,900 square miles) and a population of 3,660,834 as of 2025.[[1]](#cite_note-population-1) The capital city is [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence").

**Tuscany** ([/ˈtʌskəni/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*TUSK-ə-nee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Toscana* [[tosˈkaːna]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian")) is an administrative [region](/wiki/Regions_of_Italy "Regions of Italy") of [central Italy](/wiki/Central_Italy "Central Italy") with an area of about 23,000 square kilometres (8,900 square miles) and a population of 3,660,834 as of 2025.[[1]](#cite_note-population-1) The capital city is [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence").

Tuscany is known for its landscapes, history, artistic legacy, and influence on [high culture](/wiki/High_culture "High culture"). It is regarded as the birthplace of the [Italian Renaissance](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance "Italian Renaissance")[[5]](#cite_note-5) and of the foundations of the Italian language. The prestige established by [Tuscan](/wiki/Tuscan_dialect "Tuscan dialect")'s use in literature by [Dante Alighieri](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri"), [Petrarch](/wiki/Petrarch "Petrarch"), [Giovanni Boccaccio](/wiki/Giovanni_Boccaccio "Giovanni Boccaccio"), [Niccolò Machiavelli](/wiki/Niccol%C3%B2_Machiavelli "Niccolò Machiavelli"), and [Francesco Guicciardini](/wiki/Francesco_Guicciardini "Francesco Guicciardini") led to its adoption as the basis for elaboration of the language of culture throughout Italy.[[6]](#cite_note-6) It has been home to many figures influential in the history of art and science, and contains well-known museums such as the [Uffizi](/wiki/Uffizi "Uffizi") and the [Palazzo Pitti](/wiki/Palazzo_Pitti "Palazzo Pitti"). Tuscany is also known for [its wines](/wiki/Tuscan_wine "Tuscan wine"), including [Chianti](/wiki/Chianti "Chianti"), [Vino Nobile di Montepulciano](/wiki/Vino_Nobile_di_Montepulciano "Vino Nobile di Montepulciano"), [Morellino di Scansano](/wiki/Morellino_di_Scansano "Morellino di Scansano"), [Brunello di Montalcino](/wiki/Brunello_di_Montalcino "Brunello di Montalcino"), and white [Vernaccia di San Gimignano](/wiki/Vernaccia_di_San_Gimignano "Vernaccia di San Gimignano"). Having a strong linguistic and cultural identity, it is sometimes considered "a nation within a nation".[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Tuscany is the second-most-popular [Italian region](/wiki/Regions_of_Italy "Regions of Italy") for travelers in Italy, after [Veneto](/wiki/Veneto "Veneto").[[8]](#cite_note-8) The main tourist spots are [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), [Pisa](/wiki/Pisa "Pisa"), [San Gimignano](/wiki/San_Gimignano "San Gimignano"), [Siena](/wiki/Siena "Siena") and [Lucca](/wiki/Lucca "Lucca").[[9]](#cite_note-ilgiunco.net-9) [Castiglione della Pescaia](/wiki/Castiglione_della_Pescaia "Castiglione della Pescaia") is the most visited seaside destination in the region,[[9]](#cite_note-ilgiunco.net-9) with seaside tourism accounting for approximately 40% of tourist arrivals. The [Maremma region](/wiki/Maremma "Maremma"), the [Chianti](/wiki/Chianti_(region) "Chianti (region)"), [Versilia](/wiki/Versilia "Versilia"), and [Val d'Orcia](/wiki/Val_d%27Orcia "Val d'Orcia") are also internationally renowned and particularly popular among travelers.

Eight Tuscan localities have been designated [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"): the [historic Centre of Florence](/wiki/Historic_Centre_of_Florence "Historic Centre of Florence") (1982); the [Cathedral square of Pisa](/wiki/Piazza_dei_Miracoli "Piazza dei Miracoli") (1987); the historical centre of [San Gimignano](/wiki/San_Gimignano "San Gimignano") (1990); the historical centre of [Siena](/wiki/Siena "Siena") (1995); the historical centre of [Pienza](/wiki/Pienza "Pienza") (1996); the [Val d'Orcia](/wiki/Val_d%27Orcia "Val d'Orcia") (2004), the [Medici Villas and Gardens](/wiki/Medici_villas "Medici villas") (2013), and [Montecatini Terme](/wiki/Montecatini_Terme "Montecatini Terme") as part of [the Great Spa Towns of Europe](/wiki/The_Great_Spa_Towns_of_Europe "The Great Spa Towns of Europe") (2021). Tuscany has over 120 protected [nature reserves](/wiki/Nature_reserve "Nature reserve"), making Tuscany and its capital Florence popular tourist destinations. In 2018, Florence alone had over 5 million arrivals, making it the world's 51st [most visited city](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_international_visitors "List of cities by international visitors").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Toscana_Thornthwaite.png/250px-Toscana_Thornthwaite.png)](/wiki/File:Toscana_Thornthwaite.png)

[Thornthwaite climate classification](/wiki/Thornthwaite_climate_classification "Thornthwaite climate classification") of Tuscany

* A: Im > 200
* B: 80 < Im < 100
* B1-B2: 20 < Im < 80
* C2: 0 < Im < 20
* C1: −33,3 < Im < 0
* D: Im < −33,3

Roughly triangular in shape, Tuscany borders the regions of [Liguria](/wiki/Liguria "Liguria") to the northwest, [Emilia-Romagna](/wiki/Emilia-Romagna "Emilia-Romagna") to the north, [Marche](/wiki/Marche "Marche") and [Umbria](/wiki/Umbria "Umbria") to the east, and [Lazio](/wiki/Lazio "Lazio") to the south and southeast. The [comune](/wiki/Comune "Comune") (municipality) of [Badia Tedalda](/wiki/Badia_Tedalda "Badia Tedalda"), in the Tuscan [Province of Arezzo](/wiki/Province_of_Arezzo "Province of Arezzo"), has an exclave named Ca' Raffaello within Emilia-Romagna.

Tuscany has a western coastline on the [Ligurian Sea](/wiki/Ligurian_Sea "Ligurian Sea") and the [Tyrrhenian Sea](/wiki/Tyrrhenian_Sea "Tyrrhenian Sea"), among which is the [Tuscan Archipelago](/wiki/Tuscan_Archipelago "Tuscan Archipelago"), of which the most significant island is [Elba](/wiki/Elba "Elba"). Tuscany has an area of approximately 23,000 square kilometres (8,900 sq mi). Surrounded and crossed by major mountain chains and with few (but fertile) plains, the region has a relief dominated by hilly country used for agriculture. Hills make up nearly two-thirds (66.5%) of the region's total area, covering 15,292 square kilometres (5,904 sq mi), and mountains (of which the highest are the [Apennines](/wiki/Apennine_Mountains "Apennine Mountains")), a further 25%, or 5,770 square kilometres (2,230 sq mi). Plains occupy 8.4% of the total area—1,930 square kilometres (750 sq mi)—mainly around the valley of the [Arno](/wiki/Arno "Arno"). Many of Tuscany's most significant cities lie on the banks of the Arno, including the capital, [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), [Empoli](/wiki/Empoli "Empoli"), and [Pisa](/wiki/Pisa "Pisa"). To the north of Florence lies the [Mugello](/wiki/Mugello "Mugello") valley, a green and fertile area surrounded by the Apennine mountains, known for its rolling hills[[11]](#cite_note-11), medieval villages, and the Mugello Circuit, one of Italy’s most famous racing tracks.

The climate is fairly mild in the coastal areas, and harsher and rainy in the interior, with considerable fluctuations in temperature between winter and summer,[[12]](#cite_note-ite1geo-12) giving the region a soil-building active freeze-thaw cycle, in part accounting for the region once having served as a key breadbasket of [ancient Rome](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Tuscany](/wiki/History_of_Tuscany "History of Tuscany")

### Apennine, Proto-Villanovan and Villanovan culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Apennine, Proto-Villanovan and Villanovan culture")]

Main articles: [Apennine culture](/wiki/Apennine_culture "Apennine culture"), [Proto-Villanovan culture](/wiki/Proto-Villanovan_culture "Proto-Villanovan culture"), and [Villanovan culture](/wiki/Villanovan_culture "Villanovan culture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Ossuari_biconici_villanoviani_da_necropoli_di_casal_di_lanza_%28vulci%29%2C_IX-VIII_sec_ac._01.JPG/250px-Ossuari_biconici_villanoviani_da_necropoli_di_casal_di_lanza_%28vulci%29%2C_IX-VIII_sec_ac._01.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ossuari_biconici_villanoviani_da_necropoli_di_casal_di_lanza_(vulci),_IX-VIII_sec_ac._01.JPG)

Cinerary urns of the [Villanovan culture](/wiki/Villanovan_culture "Villanovan culture")

The pre-Etruscan history of the area in the middle and late [Bronze](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") parallels that of the [archaic](/wiki/Archaic_period_in_Greece "Archaic period in Greece") [Greeks](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarkerRasmussen20005-14) The Tuscan area was inhabited by peoples of the so-called [Apennine culture](/wiki/Apennine_culture "Apennine culture") in the [second millennium BC](/wiki/Second_millennium_BC "Second millennium BC") (c. 1400 – c. 1150 BC) who had trading relationships with the [Minoan](/wiki/Minoan_civilization "Minoan civilization") and [Mycenaean](/wiki/Mycenaean_Greece "Mycenaean Greece") civilizations in the [Aegean Sea](/wiki/Aegean_Sea "Aegean Sea"),[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarkerRasmussen20005-14) and, at the end of the Bronze Age, by peoples of the so-called [Proto-Villanovan culture](/wiki/Proto-Villanovan_culture "Proto-Villanovan culture") (c. 1100 – c. 900 BC) part of the central European [Urnfield culture](/wiki/Urnfield_culture "Urnfield culture") system. Following this, at the beginning of the [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age"), the [Villanovan culture](/wiki/Villanovan_culture "Villanovan culture") (c. 900 – c. 700 BC), regarded as the oldest phase of [Etruscan civilization](/wiki/Etruscan_civilization "Etruscan civilization"),[[15]](#cite_note-Neri-15)[[16]](#cite_note-Bartolonivillanoviana-16) saw Tuscany, and the rest of Etruria, taken over by [chiefdoms](/wiki/Chiefdom "Chiefdom").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarkerRasmussen20005-14) [City-states](/wiki/City-state "City-state") developed in the late Villanovan (paralleling Greece and the Aegean) before "[Orientalization](/wiki/Orientalizing_period "Orientalizing period")" occurred.[[17]](#cite_note-Smith2014-17)

### Etruscans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Etruscans")]

Main article: [Etruscan civilization](/wiki/Etruscan_civilization "Etruscan civilization")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Chimera_d%27arezzo%2C_fi%2C_03.JPG/250px-Chimera_d%27arezzo%2C_fi%2C_03.JPG)](/wiki/File:Chimera_d%27arezzo,_fi,_03.JPG)

The *[Chimera of Arezzo](/wiki/Chimera_of_Arezzo "Chimera of Arezzo")*, Etruscan bronze, 400 BC

The [Etruscans](/wiki/Etruscan_civilization "Etruscan civilization") (Latin: *Tusci*) created the first major [civilization](/wiki/Civilization "Civilization") in this region, large enough to establish a transport infrastructure, to implement agriculture and mining and to produce vibrant art.[[18]](#cite_note-Jones_2-18) The Etruscans lived in the area of [Etruria](/wiki/Etruria "Etruria") well into prehistory.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarkerRasmussen20005-14) The civilization grew to fill the area between the Arno and [Tiber](/wiki/Tiber "Tiber") from the tenth century BCE, reaching its peak during the seventh and sixth centuries B.C., finally succumbing to the [Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") by the first century BCE.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarkerRasmussen20001-19) From the Etruscans, Tuscany took the name of Etruria, Tuscia for the [Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") and subsequently Tuscania and Tuscany. While the areas of north-western Tuscany were inhabited by the ancient [Ligures](/wiki/Ligures "Ligures"). In northwestern Tuscany, the area between the Arno and Magra rivers was culturally aligned with the Etruscans in the early Iron Age, and came under Ligurian control in the late Iron Age.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

One reason for the eventual demise of this civilization was the increasing absorption by surrounding cultures, including the adoption of the Etruscan upper class by the Romans.[[18]](#cite_note-Jones_2-18)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarkerRasmussen20001-19)

### Romans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Romans")]

Soon after absorbing Etruria (to the north, northeast, east, and a strip to the south), Rome established the cities of [Lucca](/wiki/Lucca "Lucca"), [Pisa](/wiki/Pisa "Pisa"), [Siena](/wiki/Siena "Siena"), and [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), endowed the area with new technologies and development, and ensured peace.[[18]](#cite_note-Jones_2-18) These developments included extensions of existing roads, the introduction of aqueducts and sewers, and the construction of many buildings, both public and private. However, many of these structures have been destroyed by erosion due to weather.[[18]](#cite_note-Jones_2-18)

The Roman civilization in the West of the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") and later [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") collapsed in the fifth century, and the region fell briefly to [barbarians](/wiki/Barbarian "Barbarian") migrating through the Empire from Eastern Europe and Central Asia of the [Goths](/wiki/Goths "Goths") (Eastern – [Ostrogoths](/wiki/Ostrogoths "Ostrogoths") and Western – [Visigoths](/wiki/Visigoths "Visigoths")), then was re-conquered by the revived [Eastern Roman Empire](/wiki/Eastern_Roman_Empire "Eastern Roman Empire") (later [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire")) under the Emperor [Justinian](/wiki/Justinian "Justinian"). In the years following 572, the [Lombards](/wiki/Lombards "Lombards") arrived and designated [Lucca](/wiki/Lucca "Lucca") the capital of their subsequent [Tuscia](/wiki/Tuscia "Tuscia").[[18]](#cite_note-Jones_2-18)

### Medieval period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Medieval period")]

See also: [Duchy of Tuscia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Tuscia "Duchy of Tuscia") and [March of Tuscany](/wiki/March_of_Tuscany "March of Tuscany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Battle_of_Montaperti.jpg/330px-Battle_of_Montaperti.jpg)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_Montaperti.jpg)

[Battle of Montaperti](/wiki/Battle_of_Montaperti "Battle of Montaperti"), 1260

[Pilgrims](/wiki/Pilgrim "Pilgrim") travelling along the [Via Francigena](/wiki/Via_Francigena "Via Francigena") between [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") and France brought wealth and development during the [medieval period](/wiki/Medieval_period "Medieval period").[[18]](#cite_note-Jones_2-18) The food and shelter required by these travellers fuelled the growth of communities around churches and taverns.[[18]](#cite_note-Jones_2-18) The conflict between the [Guelphs and Ghibellines](/wiki/Guelphs_and_Ghibellines "Guelphs and Ghibellines"), factions supporting the [Papacy](/wiki/Papacy "Papacy") or the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") in central and northern Italy during the 12th and 13th centuries, split the Tuscan people.[[18]](#cite_note-Jones_2-18) The two factions gave rise to several powerful and rich [medieval communes](/wiki/Medieval_commune "Medieval commune") in Tuscany: [Arezzo](/wiki/Arezzo "Arezzo"), [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), [Lucca](/wiki/Republic_of_Lucca "Republic of Lucca"), [Pisa](/wiki/Pisa "Pisa"), and [Siena](/wiki/Siena "Siena").[[18]](#cite_note-Jones_2-18) Balance between these communes was ensured by the assets they held: Pisa, a port; Siena, banking; and Lucca, banking and silk.[[21]](#cite_note-Jones_3-21) But by the time of the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), Florence had become the cultural capital of Tuscany.[[21]](#cite_note-Jones_3-21)

One family that benefitted from Florence's growing wealth and power was the ruling [Medici family](/wiki/Medici_family "Medici family"). Its scion [Lorenzo de' Medici](/wiki/Lorenzo_de%27_Medici "Lorenzo de' Medici") was one of the most famous of the Medici. The legacy of his influence is visible today in the prodigious expression of art and architecture in Florence. His famous descendant [Catherine de' Medici](/wiki/Catherine_de%27_Medici "Catherine de' Medici") married Prince Henry (later [King Henry II](/wiki/Henry_II_of_France "Henry II of France")) of France in 1533.

The [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death") epidemic hit Tuscany starting in 1348.[[22]](#cite_note-22) It eventually killed 70% of the Tuscan population.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) According to Melissa Snell, "Florence lost a third of its population in the first six months of the plague, and from 45% to 75% of its population in the first year."[[25]](#cite_note-25) In 1630, Florence and Tuscany were once again ravaged by the [plague](/wiki/1629%E2%80%9331_Italian_plague "1629–31 Italian plague").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Renaissance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Renaissance")]

Further information: [The Renaissance](/wiki/The_Renaissance "The Renaissance") and [Italian Renaissance](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance "Italian Renaissance")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Botticelli-primavera.jpg/250px-Botticelli-primavera.jpg)](/wiki/File:Botticelli-primavera.jpg)

*[Primavera](/wiki/Primavera_(Botticelli) "Primavera (Botticelli)")* (1482) by [Botticelli](/wiki/Sandro_Botticelli "Sandro Botticelli")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Hanging_and_burning_of_Girolamo_Savonarola_in_Florence.jpg/250px-Hanging_and_burning_of_Girolamo_Savonarola_in_Florence.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hanging_and_burning_of_Girolamo_Savonarola_in_Florence.jpg)

Hanging and burning of [Girolamo Savonarola](/wiki/Girolamo_Savonarola "Girolamo Savonarola") in [Piazza della Signoria](/wiki/Piazza_della_Signoria "Piazza della Signoria") in [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence") 1498 — painting depicting Renaissance Florence

Tuscany, especially [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), is regarded as the birthplace of the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"). Though "Tuscany" remained a linguistic, cultural, and geographic conception rather than a political reality, in the 15th century, Florence extended its dominion in Tuscany through the annexation of Arezzo in 1384, the purchase of Pisa in 1405, and the suppression of a local resistance there (1406). [Livorno](/wiki/Livorno "Livorno") was bought in 1421 and became the harbour of Florence.

From the leading city of Florence, the republic was from 1434 onward dominated by the increasingly monarchical [Medici](/wiki/Medici "Medici") family. Initially, under [Cosimo](/wiki/Cosimo_de%27_Medici "Cosimo de' Medici"), [Piero the Gouty](/wiki/Piero_di_Cosimo_de%27_Medici "Piero di Cosimo de' Medici"), [Lorenzo](/wiki/Lorenzo_de%27_Medici "Lorenzo de' Medici") and [Piero the Unfortunate](/wiki/Piero_the_Unfortunate "Piero the Unfortunate"), the forms of the republic were retained and the Medici ruled without a title, usually without even a formal office. These rulers presided over the [Florentine Renaissance](/wiki/Florentine_Renaissance "Florentine Renaissance"). There was a return to the republic from 1494 to 1512, when first [Girolamo Savonarola](/wiki/Girolamo_Savonarola "Girolamo Savonarola") then [Piero Soderini](/wiki/Piero_Soderini "Piero Soderini") oversaw the state. Cardinal Giovanni di Lorenzo de' Medici retook the city with Spanish forces in 1512, before going to Rome to become [Pope Leo X](/wiki/Pope_Leo_X "Pope Leo X"). Florence was dominated by a series of papal proxies until 1527 when the citizens declared the republic again, only to have it taken from them again in 1530 after a siege by an Imperial and Spanish army. At this point [Pope Clement VII](/wiki/Pope_Clement_VII "Pope Clement VII") and [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor") appointed [Alessandro de' Medici](/wiki/Alessandro_de%27_Medici "Alessandro de' Medici") as the first formal hereditary ruler.

The Sienese commune was not incorporated into Tuscany until 1555, and during the 15th century, Siena enjoyed a cultural 'Sienese Renaissance' with its own more conservative character. Lucca remained an independent republic until 1847 when it became part of [Grand Duchy of Tuscany](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Tuscany "Grand Duchy of Tuscany") by the will of its people. [Piombino](/wiki/Piombino "Piombino") and other strategic towns constituted the tiny [State of the *Presidi*](/wiki/State_of_the_Presidi "State of the Presidi") under Spanish control.

### Modern era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Modern era")]

See also: [Grand Duchy of Tuscany](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Tuscany "Grand Duchy of Tuscany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/GrhToscane.jpg/250px-GrhToscane.jpg)](/wiki/File:GrhToscane.jpg)

Map of the [Grand Duchy of Tuscany](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Tuscany "Grand Duchy of Tuscany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Ossario_sant_anna_di_stazzema.jpg/250px-Ossario_sant_anna_di_stazzema.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ossario_sant_anna_di_stazzema.jpg)

Memorial to the victims of the [Sant'Anna di Stazzema massacre](/wiki/Sant%27Anna_di_Stazzema_massacre "Sant'Anna di Stazzema massacre"), in which 560 locals were murdered by [Nazi Germans](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and [Italian Fascists](/wiki/Black_Brigades "Black Brigades") in 1944

In the 16th century, the [Medicis](/wiki/House_of_Medici "House of Medici"), rulers of Florence, annexed the [Republic of Siena](/wiki/Republic_of_Siena "Republic of Siena"), creating the [Grand Duchy of Tuscany](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Tuscany "Grand Duchy of Tuscany"). The Medici family became extinct in 1737 with the death of [Gian Gastone](/wiki/Gian_Gastone_de%27_Medici,_Grand_Duke_of_Tuscany "Gian Gastone de' Medici, Grand Duke of Tuscany"), and Tuscany was transferred to [Francis](/wiki/Francis_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Francis I, Holy Roman Emperor"), [Duke of Lorraine](/wiki/Duke_of_Lorraine "Duke of Lorraine") and husband of Austrian Empress [Maria Theresa](/wiki/Maria_Theresa "Maria Theresa"), who let the country be ruled by his son. The dynasty of the Lorena ruled Tuscany until 1860, except during the [Napoleonic period](/wiki/Napoleonic_period "Napoleonic period"), when most of the country was annexed to the [French Empire](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire"). After the [Second Italian War of Independence](/wiki/Second_Italian_War_of_Independence "Second Italian War of Independence"), a revolution evicted the last Grand Duke, and after a [plebiscite](/wiki/Plebiscite "Plebiscite"), Tuscany became part of the new [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy"). From 1864 to 1870 Florence became the second capital of the kingdom.

Under [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini"), the area came under the dominance of local [Fascist](/wiki/National_Fascist_Party "National Fascist Party") leaders such as [Dino Perrone Compagni](/wiki/Dino_Perrone_Compagni "Dino Perrone Compagni") (from Florence), and [Costanzo](/wiki/Costanzo_Ciano "Costanzo Ciano") and [Galeazzo Ciano](/wiki/Galeazzo_Ciano "Galeazzo Ciano") (from [Livorno](/wiki/Livorno "Livorno")). Following the fall of Mussolini and the armistice of 8 September 1943, Tuscany became part of the Nazi-controlled [Italian Social Republic](/wiki/Italian_Social_Republic "Italian Social Republic") and was conquered almost totally by the Anglo-American forces during the summer of 1944.

Following the end of the Social Republic and the transition from the Kingdom to the modern Italian Republic, Tuscany once more flourished as a cultural centre of Italy. Since the establishment of the regional government in 1970, Tuscany has always been ruled by centre-left governments.

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Culture")]

See also: [List of museums in Tuscany](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_Tuscany "List of museums in Tuscany")

Tuscany has an immense cultural and artistic heritage, expressed in the region's churches, palaces, art galleries, museums, villages, and piazzas. Many of these artifacts are found in the main cities, such as [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence") and [Siena](/wiki/Siena "Siena"), but also in smaller villages scattered around the region, such as [San Gimignano](/wiki/San_Gimignano "San Gimignano").

### Art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Art")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/%27David%27_by_Michelangelo_Fir_JBU005_denoised.jpg/250px-%27David%27_by_Michelangelo_Fir_JBU005_denoised.jpg)](/wiki/File:%27David%27_by_Michelangelo_Fir_JBU005_denoised.jpg)

[Michelangelo's *David*](/wiki/David_(Michelangelo) "David (Michelangelo)")

Tuscany has a unique artistic legacy, and Florence is one of the world's most important water-colour centres, even so that it is often nicknamed the "art palace of Italy" (the region is also believed to have the largest concentration of Renaissance art and architecture in the world).[[27]](#cite_note-27) Painters such as [Cimabue](/wiki/Cimabue "Cimabue") and [Giotto](/wiki/Giotto "Giotto"), the fathers of Italian painting, lived in Florence and Tuscany, as well as Arnolfo and [Andrea Pisano](/wiki/Andrea_Pisano "Andrea Pisano"), renewers of architecture and sculpture; [Brunelleschi](/wiki/Filippo_Brunelleschi "Filippo Brunelleschi"), [Donatello](/wiki/Donatello "Donatello") and [Masaccio](/wiki/Masaccio "Masaccio"), forefathers of the Renaissance; [Ghiberti](/wiki/Lorenzo_Ghiberti "Lorenzo Ghiberti") and the [Della Robbias](/wiki/Della_Robbia "Della Robbia"), [Filippo Lippi](/wiki/Filippo_Lippi "Filippo Lippi") and [Angelico](/wiki/Fra_Angelico "Fra Angelico"); [Botticelli](/wiki/Botticelli "Botticelli"), [Paolo Uccello](/wiki/Paolo_Uccello "Paolo Uccello"), and the universal genius of [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci") and [Michelangelo](/wiki/Michelangelo "Michelangelo").[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The region contains numerous museums and art galleries, many housing some of the world's most precious works of art. Such museums include the [Uffizi](/wiki/Uffizi "Uffizi"), which keeps Botticelli's *[The Birth of Venus](/wiki/The_Birth_of_Venus "The Birth of Venus")*, the [Palazzo Pitti](/wiki/Palazzo_Pitti "Palazzo Pitti"), and the [Bargello](/wiki/Bargello "Bargello"), to name a few. Most of the frescos, sculptures, and paintings in Tuscany are held in the region's abundant churches and cathedrals, such as [Florence Cathedral](/wiki/Florence_Cathedral "Florence Cathedral"), [Siena Cathedral](/wiki/Siena_Cathedral "Siena Cathedral"), [Pisa Cathedral](/wiki/Pisa_Cathedral "Pisa Cathedral") and the [Collegiata di San Gimignano](/wiki/Collegiata_di_San_Gimignano "Collegiata di San Gimignano").

### Art schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Art schools")]

Main articles: [Florentine painting](/wiki/Florentine_painting "Florentine painting"), [Sienese School](/wiki/Sienese_School "Sienese School"), and [Lucchese School](/wiki/Lucchese_School "Lucchese School")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Pietro_lorenzetti%2C_compianto_%28dettaglio%29_basilica_inferiore_di_assisi_%281310-1329%29.jpg/250px-Pietro_lorenzetti%2C_compianto_%28dettaglio%29_basilica_inferiore_di_assisi_%281310-1329%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pietro_lorenzetti,_compianto_(dettaglio)_basilica_inferiore_di_assisi_(1310-1329).jpg)

A painting from the [Sienese School](/wiki/Sienese_School "Sienese School") by [Pietro Lorenzetti](/wiki/Pietro_Lorenzetti "Pietro Lorenzetti")

In the medieval period and the Renaissance, four main Tuscan art schools competed against each other: the [Florentine School](/wiki/Florentine_School "Florentine School"), the [Sienese School](/wiki/Sienese_School "Sienese School"), the Pisan School, and the [Lucchese School](/wiki/Lucchese_School "Lucchese School").

* The Florentine School refers to artists in, from, or influenced by the [naturalistic](/wiki/Realism_(arts) "Realism (arts)") style developed in the 14th century, largely through the efforts of [Giotto di Bondone](/wiki/Giotto_di_Bondone "Giotto di Bondone"), and in the 15th century the leading school of the world. Some of the best known artists of the Florentine School are [Brunelleschi](/wiki/Filippo_Brunelleschi "Filippo Brunelleschi"), [Donatello](/wiki/Donatello "Donatello"), [Michelangelo](/wiki/Michelangelo "Michelangelo"), [Fra Angelico](/wiki/Fra_Angelico "Fra Angelico"), [Botticelli](/wiki/Botticelli "Botticelli"), [Lippi](/wiki/Filippo_Lippi "Filippo Lippi"), [Masolino](/wiki/Masolino_da_Panicale "Masolino da Panicale"), and [Masaccio](/wiki/Masaccio "Masaccio").
* The Sienese School of painting flourished in [Siena](/wiki/Siena "Siena") between the 13th and 15th centuries and for a time rivaled Florence, though it was more conservative, being inclined towards the decorative beauty and elegant grace of late [Gothic art](/wiki/Gothic_art "Gothic art"). Its most important representatives include [Duccio](/wiki/Duccio "Duccio"), whose work shows Byzantine influence; his pupil [Simone Martini](/wiki/Simone_Martini "Simone Martini"); [Pietro](/wiki/Pietro_Lorenzetti "Pietro Lorenzetti") and [Ambrogio Lorenzetti](/wiki/Ambrogio_Lorenzetti "Ambrogio Lorenzetti"); [Domenico](/wiki/Domenico_di_Bartolo "Domenico di Bartolo") and [Taddeo di Bartolo](/wiki/Taddeo_di_Bartolo "Taddeo di Bartolo"); and [Sassetta](/wiki/Stefano_di_Giovanni "Stefano di Giovanni") and [Matteo di Giovanni](/wiki/Matteo_di_Giovanni "Matteo di Giovanni"). Unlike the naturalistic Florentine art, there is a mystical streak in Sienese art,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] characterized by a common focus on miraculous events, distortions of time and place, and often dreamlike coloration, with less attention to proportions. In the 16th century, the Mannerists [Beccafumi](/wiki/Domenico_di_Pace_Beccafumi "Domenico di Pace Beccafumi") and [Il Sodoma](/wiki/Il_Sodoma "Il Sodoma") worked there. While Baldassare Peruzzi was born and trained in Siena, his major works and style reflect his long career in Rome. The economic and political decline of Siena by the 16th century, and its eventual subjugation by Florence, largely checked the development of Sienese painting, although it also meant that many Sienese works in churches and public buildings were not discarded or destroyed by new paintings or rebuilding. Siena remains a remarkably well-preserved Italian late-Medieval town.
* The Lucchese School, also known as the School of Lucca and as the Pisan-Lucchese School, was a school of painting and sculpture that flourished in the 11th and 12th centuries in the western and southern part of the region, with an important centre in [Volterra](/wiki/Volterra "Volterra"). The art is mostly anonymous. Although not as elegant or delicate as the Florentine School, Lucchese works are remarkable for their monumentality.

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Language")]

Main article: [Tuscan dialect](/wiki/Tuscan_dialect "Tuscan dialect")

Apart from Tuscan being the standard model for Italy's national language (historically preferred over other languages of the peninsula), the [Tuscan dialect](/wiki/Tuscan_dialect "Tuscan dialect") (*dialetto toscano*) is spoken in Tuscany. The Italian language is actually literary Tuscan itself, specifically the [Florentine dialect](/wiki/Florentine_dialect "Florentine dialect"), only commonly referred to as Italian for political and nationalist reasons. It became the language of culture for all the people of Italy, thanks to the prestige of the masterpieces of [Dante Alighieri](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri"), [Petrarch](/wiki/Petrarch "Petrarch"), [Giovanni Boccaccio](/wiki/Giovanni_Boccaccio "Giovanni Boccaccio"), [Niccolò Machiavelli](/wiki/Niccol%C3%B2_Machiavelli "Niccolò Machiavelli"), and [Francesco Guicciardini](/wiki/Francesco_Guicciardini "Francesco Guicciardini"). It would later become the official language of all the [Italian states](/wiki/List_of_historic_states_of_Italy "List of historic states of Italy") and of the Kingdom of Italy, when it was formed. Many Tuscan terms are also common in the [Central Italian](/wiki/Central_Italian "Central Italian") dialects of Umbria and some parts of [Emilia Romagna](/wiki/Emilia-Romagna "Emilia-Romagna").[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Music")]

Main article: [Music of Tuscany](/wiki/Music_of_Tuscany "Music of Tuscany")

See also: [Music of Florence](/wiki/Music_of_Florence "Music of Florence")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/GiacomoPuccini.jpg/250px-GiacomoPuccini.jpg)](/wiki/File:GiacomoPuccini.jpg)

[Giacomo Puccini](/wiki/Giacomo_Puccini "Giacomo Puccini")

Tuscany has a rich ancient and modern musical tradition, and has produced numerous composers and musicians, including [Giacomo Puccini](/wiki/Giacomo_Puccini "Giacomo Puccini") and [Pietro Mascagni](/wiki/Pietro_Mascagni "Pietro Mascagni"). Florence is the main musical centre of Tuscany. The city was at the heart of much of the Western musical tradition. It was there that the [Florentine Camerata](/wiki/Florentine_Camerata "Florentine Camerata") convened in the mid-16th century and experimented with setting tales of [Greek mythology](/wiki/Greek_mythology "Greek mythology") to music and staging, resulting in the first operas, fostering the further development of the operatic form, and the later developments of separate "classical" forms such as the [symphony](/wiki/Symphony "Symphony").

There are numerous musical centres in Tuscany. [Arezzo](/wiki/Arezzo "Arezzo") is indelibly connected with the name of [Guido d'Arezzo](/wiki/Guido_d%27Arezzo "Guido d'Arezzo"), the 11th-century monk who invented modern [musical notation](/wiki/Musical_notation "Musical notation") and the [do-re-mi](/wiki/Solf%C3%A8ge "Solfège") system of naming notes of the scale; [Lucca](/wiki/Lucca "Lucca") hosted possibly the greatest Italian composer of [Verismo](/wiki/Verismo "Verismo"), [Giacomo Puccini](/wiki/Giacomo_Puccini "Giacomo Puccini") together with [Alfredo Catalani](/wiki/Alfredo_Catalani "Alfredo Catalani"), while [Pietro Mascagni](/wiki/Pietro_Mascagni "Pietro Mascagni") was born in [Livorno](/wiki/Livorno "Livorno"); and [Siena](/wiki/Siena "Siena") is well known for the [Accademia Musicale Chigiana](/wiki/Accademia_Musicale_Chigiana "Accademia Musicale Chigiana"), an organization that currently sponsors major musical activities such as the Siena Music Week and the Alfredo Casella International Composition Competition. Other important musical centres in Tuscany include [Pisa](/wiki/Pisa "Pisa") and [Grosseto](/wiki/Grosseto "Grosseto").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Literature")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Francesco_Petrarca00.jpg/250px-Francesco_Petrarca00.jpg)](/wiki/File:Francesco_Petrarca00.jpg)

Tuscan poet and literary figure [Petrarch](/wiki/Petrarch "Petrarch")

Several famous writers and poets are from Tuscany, most notably Florentine author [Dante Alighieri](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri"). Tuscany's literary scene particularly thrived in the 13th century and the Renaissance.

In Tuscany, especially in the Middle Ages, popular love poetry existed. A school of imitators of the Sicilians was led by [Dante da Maiano](/wiki/Dante_da_Maiano "Dante da Maiano"), but its literary originality took another line – that of humorous and satirical poetry. The democratic form of government created a style of poetry that stood strongly against the medieval mystic and chivalrous style. Devout invocation of God or a lady came from the [cloister](/wiki/Cloister "Cloister") and the castle; in the streets of the cities everything that had gone before was treated with ridicule or biting [sarcasm](/wiki/Sarcasm "Sarcasm"). [Folgóre da San Gimignano](/wiki/Folg%C3%B3re_da_San_Gimignano "Folgóre da San Gimignano") laughs when in his sonnets he tells a party of Sienese youths the occupations of every month in the year, or when he teaches a party of Florentine lads the pleasures of every day in the week. Cenne della Chitarra laughs when he parodies Folgore's sonnets. The sonnets of Rustico di Filippo are half-fun and half-satire, as is the work of [Cecco Angiolieri](/wiki/Cecco_Angiolieri "Cecco Angiolieri") of Siena, the oldest humorist we[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] know, a far-off precursor of [François Rabelais](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Rabelais "François Rabelais") and [Michel de Montaigne](/wiki/Michel_de_Montaigne "Michel de Montaigne").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Another type of poetry also began in Tuscany. Guittone d'Arezzo made art abandon chivalry and [Provençal](/wiki/Provence "Provence") forms for national motives and Latin forms. He attempted political poetry, and although his work is often obscure, he prepared the way for the Bolognese school. [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna") was the city of science, and philosophical poetry appeared there. [Guido Guinizelli](/wiki/Guido_Guinizelli "Guido Guinizelli") was the poet after the new fashion of the art. In his work, the ideas of [chivalry](/wiki/Chivalry "Chivalry") are changed and enlarged. Only those whose heart is pure can be blessed with true love, regardless of class. He refuted the traditional credo of [courtly love](/wiki/Courtly_love "Courtly love"), for which love is a subtle philosophy only a few chosen knights and princesses could grasp. Love is blind to blasons but not to a good heart when it finds one: when it succeeds it is the result of the spiritual, not physical affinity between two souls. Guinizzelli's democratic view can be better understood in the light of the greater equality and freedom enjoyed by the city-states of the center-north and the rise of a middle class eager to legitimise itself in the eyes of the old nobility, still regarded with respect and admiration but dispossessed of its political power.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Guinizelli's *[Canzoni](/wiki/Canzone "Canzone")* make up the bible of [Dolce Stil Novo](/wiki/Dolce_Stil_Novo "Dolce Stil Novo"), and one in particular, "Al cor gentil" ("To a Kind Heart") is considered[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] the manifesto of the new movement which would bloom in Florence under [Cavalcanti](/wiki/Guido_Cavalcanti "Guido Cavalcanti"), Dante and their followers. His poetry has some of the faults of the school of d'Arezzo. Nevertheless, he marks a great development in the history of Italian art, especially because of his close connection with Dante's [lyric poetry](/wiki/Lyric_poetry "Lyric poetry").

In the 13th century, there were several major [allegorical poems](/wiki/Allegory "Allegory"). One of these is by [Brunetto Latini](/wiki/Brunetto_Latini "Brunetto Latini"), who was a close friend of Dante. His *Tesoretto* is a short poem, in seven-syllable verses, rhyming in couplets, in which the author professes to be lost in a wilderness and to meet with a lady, who represents Nature, from whom he receives much instruction. We see here the vision, the allegory, the instruction with a moral object, three elements which we shall find again in the *Divine Comedy*. [Francesco da Barberino](/wiki/Francesco_da_Barberino "Francesco da Barberino"), a learned lawyer who was secretary to bishops, a judge, and a [notary](/wiki/Notary_public "Notary public"), wrote two little allegorical poems, the *Documenti d'amore* and *Del reggimento e dei costumi delle donne*. The poems today are generally studied not as literature, but for historical context. A fourth allegorical work was the *Intelligenza*, which is sometimes attributed to Compagni but is probably only a translation of French poems.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

In the 15th century, [humanist](/wiki/Renaissance_humanism "Renaissance humanism") and publisher [Aldus Manutius](/wiki/Aldus_Manutius "Aldus Manutius") published the Tuscan poets Petrarch and Dante Alighieri (*[Divine Comedy](/wiki/Divine_Comedy "Divine Comedy")*), creating the model for what became a standard for modern Italian.

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

Main article: [Tuscan food](/wiki/Tuscan_food "Tuscan food")

See also: [Tuscan wine](/wiki/Tuscan_wine "Tuscan wine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Salumi_e_vino_lucchese.JPG/330px-Salumi_e_vino_lucchese.JPG)](/wiki/File:Salumi_e_vino_lucchese.JPG)

An assortment of Tuscan foods: various wine and cheese, and different sorts of salamis and hams

Simplicity is central to [Tuscan cuisine](/wiki/Tuscan_food "Tuscan food"). [Legumes](/wiki/Legume "Legume"), bread, cheese, vegetables, mushrooms, and fresh fruit are used. [Olive oil](/wiki/Olive_oil "Olive oil") is made from Moraiolo, [Leccino](/wiki/Leccino "Leccino") and Frantoiano olives. White [truffles](/wiki/Truffle "Truffle") from [San Miniato](/wiki/San_Miniato "San Miniato") appear in October and November. Beef of the highest quality comes from the [Chiana Valley](/wiki/Chiana_Valley "Chiana Valley"), specifically, a breed known as [Chianina](/wiki/Chianina "Chianina") used for [Florentine steak](/wiki/Florentine_steak "Florentine steak"). The indigenous [Cinta Senese](/wiki/Cinta_Senese "Cinta Senese") breed of pork is also produced.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Wine is a famous and common produce of Tuscany. The red wine [Chianti](/wiki/Chianti "Chianti") is perhaps the most well-known internationally. Due to the many British tourists who come to the area where Chianti wine is produced this specific area has been nicknamed "[Chiantishire](/wiki/Chiantishire "Chiantishire")".

### Postage stamps

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Postage stamps")]

Main article: [Postage stamps and postal history of Tuscany](/wiki/Postage_stamps_and_postal_history_of_Tuscany "Postage stamps and postal history of Tuscany")

Between 1851 and 1860, the [Grand Duchy of Tuscany](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Tuscany "Grand Duchy of Tuscany"), an independent Italian state until 1859 when it joined the [United Provinces of Central Italy](/wiki/United_Provinces_of_Central_Italy "United Provinces of Central Italy"), produced two postage stamp issues which are among the most prized [classic stamp](/wiki/Classic_stamp "Classic stamp") issues of the world, and include the most valuable Italian stamp. The Grand Duchy of Tuscany was an independent Italian state from 1569 to 1859 but was occupied by France from 1808 to 1814. The Duchy comprised most of the present area of Tuscany, and its capital was Florence. In December 1859, the Grand Duchy officially ceased to exist, being joined to the duchies of [Modena](/wiki/Duchy_of_Modena_and_Reggio "Duchy of Modena and Reggio") and [Parma](/wiki/Duchy_of_Parma "Duchy of Parma") to form the United Provinces of Central Italy, which was annexed by the [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia "Kingdom of Sardinia") a few months later in March 1860. In 1862 it became part of Italy and joined the Italian postal system.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Economy")]

The gross domestic product (GDP) of the region was 117.5 billion euros in 2018, accounting for 6.7% of Italy's economic output. GDP per capita adjusted for purchasing power was 31,400 euros or 104% of the EU27 average in the same year. The GDP per employee was 106% of the EU average.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Unemployment rate")]

### Agriculture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Agriculture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Autunno_in_Chianti_Toscana.jpg/330px-Autunno_in_Chianti_Toscana.jpg)](/wiki/File:Autunno_in_Chianti_Toscana.jpg)

Vineyards in the [Chianti](/wiki/Chianti "Chianti") region

The subsoil in Tuscany is relatively rich in mineral resources, with iron ore, copper, mercury, and [lignite](/wiki/Lignite "Lignite") mines, the famous *soffioni* ([fumarole](/wiki/Fumarole "Fumarole")) at [Larderello](/wiki/Larderello "Larderello"), and the vast marble mines in [Versilia](/wiki/Versilia "Versilia"). Although its share is falling all the time, agriculture still contributes to the region's economy. In the region's inland areas cereals, potatoes, olives, and grapes are grown. The swamplands, which used to be marshy, now produce vegetables, rice, tobacco, beets, and sunflowers.[[12]](#cite_note-ite1geo-12)

### Industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Industry")]

One of the traditional foundations of the industrial sector is mining, given the abundance of underground resources. Also of note are textiles and clothing, chemicals and pharmaceuticals, steel and metalworking, glass and ceramics, and printing and publishing. Smaller areas specialising in manufacturing and craft industries are found in the hinterland: the leather and footwear area in the south-west part of the province of Florence, the hot-house plant area in [Pistoia](/wiki/Pistoia "Pistoia"), the ceramics and textile industries in the [Prato](/wiki/Prato "Prato") area, scooters, and motorcycles in [Pontedera](/wiki/Pontedera "Pontedera"), and the processing of timber for the manufacture of wooden furniture in the [Cascina](/wiki/Cascina "Cascina") area. The heavy industries (mining, steel, and mechanical engineering) are concentrated along the coastal strip ([Livorno](/wiki/Livorno "Livorno") and [Pisa](/wiki/Pisa "Pisa") areas), where there are also important chemical industries. Also of note are the marble ([Carrara](/wiki/Carrara "Carrara") area) and paper industries ([Lucca](/wiki/Lucca "Lucca") area).[[12]](#cite_note-ite1geo-12)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Tourism")]

In Tuscany 80% of tourism demand is concentrated in cities of art and seaside resorts; the rest is divided between the countryside, hills, and mountains. In 2019 the municipalities with the relatively higher percentage of presences, in descending order, are: [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), [Pisa](/wiki/Pisa "Pisa"), [Montecatini Terme](/wiki/Montecatini_Terme "Montecatini Terme"), [Castiglione della Pescaia](/wiki/Castiglione_della_Pescaia "Castiglione della Pescaia"), [San Vincenzo](/wiki/San_Vincenzo,_Tuscany "San Vincenzo, Tuscany"), [Orbetello](/wiki/Orbetello "Orbetello"), [Grosseto](/wiki/Grosseto "Grosseto"), [Siena](/wiki/Siena "Siena"), [Bibbona](/wiki/Bibbona "Bibbona"), [Viareggio](/wiki/Viareggio "Viareggio"), [Capoliveri](/wiki/Capoliveri "Capoliveri"). In the Tuscan tourist areas, together with the Florentine area, the Etruscan Coast holds the relatively largest share of presences, immediately followed by [Maremma](/wiki/Maremma "Maremma") ([Monte Argentario](/wiki/Monte_Argentario "Monte Argentario"), [Giglio Island](/wiki/Giglio_Island "Giglio Island"), [Talamone](/wiki/Talamone "Talamone"), [Capalbio](/wiki/Capalbio "Capalbio")), Terre di Pisa and [Elba](/wiki/Elba "Elba").[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Tuscany has many small and picturesque villages; 29 of them have been selected by *[I Borghi più belli d'Italia](/wiki/I_Borghi_pi%C3%B9_belli_d%27Italia "I Borghi più belli d'Italia")* (English: The most beautiful Villages of Italy),[[37]](#cite_note-37) a non-profit private association of small Italian towns of strong historical and artistic interest[[38]](#cite_note-38) that was founded on the initiative of the Tourism Council of the National Association of Italian Municipalities.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

See also: [Fashion in Florence](/wiki/Fashion_in_Florence "Fashion in Florence") and [Fashion designers of Florence](/wiki/Fashion_designers_of_Florence "Fashion designers of Florence")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Via_de_Tornabuoni_03.JPG/250px-Via_de_Tornabuoni_03.JPG)](/wiki/File:Via_de_Tornabuoni_03.JPG)

The [Via de' Tornabuoni](/wiki/Via_de%27_Tornabuoni "Via de' Tornabuoni") in Florence, the city's top fashion and shopping street, contains some of the world's most luxurious clothing and jewelry houses, such as [Cartier](/wiki/Cartier_(jeweler) "Cartier (jeweler)"), [Ferragamo](/wiki/Salvatore_Ferragamo_S.p.A. "Salvatore Ferragamo S.p.A."), [Gucci](/wiki/Gucci "Gucci"), [Versace](/wiki/Versace "Versace") and [Bulgari](/wiki/Bulgari "Bulgari").

The fashion and textile industry are the pillars of the Florentine economy. In the 15th century, Florentines were working with luxury textiles such as wool and silk. Today the greatest designers in Europe utilize the textile industry in Tuscany, and especially Florence. A large plant of production is happening in the town of [Prato](/wiki/Prato "Prato").

Italy has one of the strongest textile industries in Europe, accounting for approximately one-quarter of European production. Its turnover is over 25 billion euros. It is the third largest supplier of clothing after China and Japan. The Italian fashion industry generates 60% of its turnover abroad.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

### Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Transport")]

The region has two main airports:

* [Pisa International Airport](/wiki/Pisa_International_Airport "Pisa International Airport") is the busiest airport in the region, with 5.5 million passengers served in 2024.[[41]](#cite_note-41)
* [Florence Airport](/wiki/Florence_Airport "Florence Airport") is the second-busiest airport. It served 3.5 million passengers in 2024.

[Marina di Campo Airport](/wiki/Marina_di_Campo_Airport "Marina di Campo Airport") is a small airport serving the island of [Elba](/wiki/Elba "Elba"). The airport provides direct routes to the Italian mainland which are operated by [Silver Air](/wiki/Silver_Air_(Czech_Republic) "Silver Air (Czech Republic)").[[42]](#cite_note-42)

The population density of Tuscany, with 159 inhabitants per square kilometre (410/sq mi) in 2025, is below the national average (195/km2 or 510/sq mi). This is due to the low population density of the provinces of Arezzo, Siena, and especially Grosseto (50/km2 or 130/sq mi). The highest density is found in the province of Prato (675/km2 or 1,750/sq mi), followed by the provinces of Pistoia, Livorno, Florence and Lucca, peaking in the cities of Florence (more than 3,500/km2 or 9,100/sq mi), Livorno, Prato, Viareggio, Forte dei Marmi and Montecatini Terme (all with a population density of more than 1,000/km2 or 2,600/sq mi). The territorial distribution of the population is closely linked to the socio-cultural and, more recently, economic and industrial development of Tuscany.[[12]](#cite_note-ite1geo-12)

Accordingly, the least densely populated areas are those where the main activity is agriculture, unlike the others where, despite the presence of several large industrial complexes, the main activities are connected with tourism and associated services, alongside many small firms in the leather, glass, paper and clothing sectors.[[12]](#cite_note-ite1geo-12)

Italians make up 93% of the total population. Starting from the 1980s, the region has attracted a large flux of immigrants, particularly from China, Romania, Albania and Morocco. There is also a significant community of British and American residents. As of 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit), the Italian national institute of statistics ISTAT estimated that 275,149 foreign-born immigrants live in Tuscany, equal to 7% of the total regional population.

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuscany&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

Main article: [Politics of Tuscany](/wiki/Politics_of_Tuscany "Politics of Tuscany")

The [President of Tuscany](/wiki/President_of_Tuscany "President of Tuscany") is the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"). [Legislative power](/wiki/Legislative_power "Legislative power") is vested in the [Regional Council of Tuscany](/wiki/Regional_Council_of_Tuscany "Regional Council of Tuscany"), while [executive power](/wiki/Executive_power "Executive power") is exercised by the Regional Government led by the President, who is directly elected by the people. The current Statute, which regulates the functioning of the regional institutions, has been in force since 2005.

Tuscany is a stronghold of the centre-left [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(Italy) "Democratic Party (Italy)") (PD), forming with [Emilia-Romagna](/wiki/Emilia-Romagna "Emilia-Romagna"), [Umbria](/wiki/Umbria "Umbria") and [Marche](/wiki/Marche "Marche") the so-called Italian political "[Red Quadrilateral](/wiki/Red_belt_(Italy) "Red belt (Italy)")". Since 1970, Tuscany has been continuously governed by left-wing governments.
